summary:
Nippur, located in what is now south-central Iraq approximately midway between the ancient cities of Babylon and Ur, was one of the most important religious centers in Mesopotamian civilization, functioning as the seat of the god Enlil — the chief of the Sumerian pantheon and lord of wind and air. The city's early settlement extends back to at least 3500 BCE, placing it within the formative period of Sumerian urbanism. Unlike many Mesopotamian cities whose importance was primarily political or military, Nippur derived its prestige from its religious centrality: it was the city whose religious endorsement legitimized the claims of rulers across Mesopotamia to universal kingship.

During the early period of its development (3500-3000 BCE), Nippur was developing the temple precinct of Ekur — the sacred mountain house dedicated to Enlil — that would become one of the holiest sites in the ancient Near East. The city's central location in the Mesopotamian alluvial plain, roughly equidistant from both Upper and Lower Mesopotamia, reinforced its symbolic role as a neutral religious capital that transcended the political rivalries of the Sumerian city-states.

Nippur would eventually become famous as a center of scribal education, with its archives yielding some of the largest collections of Sumerian literary and administrative tablets ever found. Thousands of school tablets, literary compositions, hymns, and administrative records recovered from Nippur have provided modern scholars with the bulk of what is known about Sumerian language, literature, and religion. The city's early development during this period laid the sacred and institutional foundations for this later intellectual prominence.
